questions.question: Explain the relationship between popen() and pclose() functions.
keyPoints: popen() creates a pipe and forks a child process to execute a command
pclose() closes the pipe stream created by popen()
pclose() waits for the child process to terminate and collects its exit status
pclose() prevents zombie processes by calling waitpid()

questions.question: What are the different modes in which popen() can be opened? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: 'r' mode - read from command's stdout, program reads the command output
'w' mode - write to command's stdin, program writes to the command
These modes control the direction of data flow through the pipe

questions.question: What is a zombie process and how does pclose() help prevent it?
keyPoints: Zombie process is a terminated process whose exit status hasn't been collected
Without pclose(), child processes become zombies after termination
pclose() internally calls waitpid() to collect the child's exit status
This prevents the creation of zombie processes and releases process table entries

questions.question: Write the prototype of pclose() function and explain its return values.
keyPoints: Prototype: int pclose(FILE *stream)
Returns termination status of command on success
Returns -1 if stream is not a pipe or if waitpid() fails
Use WIFEXITED and WEXITSTATUS macros to extract exit code

questions.question: Explain what happens if pclose() is not called after popen().
keyPoints: The child process becomes a zombie (defunct) process
File descriptors may leak causing resource exhaustion
Memory leaks may occur in the kernel
System may run out of available file descriptors or process slots

questions.question: How do you extract the exit code from the value returned by pclose()?
keyPoints: Check if process terminated normally using WIFEXITED(status)
Extract exit code using WEXITSTATUS(status) macro
Example: if (WIFEXITED(status)) exit_code = WEXITSTATUS(status)
This gives the actual return value of the command (0-255)

questions.question: What are the advantages of using popen()/pclose() over traditional pipe()+fork()+exec()?
keyPoints: Simpler API - single function call instead of multiple
Automatically handles shell expansion and command parsing
Automatically handles process creation and pipe setup
Automatically prevents zombie processes via pclose()

questions.question: Write a C program that uses popen() to execute the 'ls -l' command and reads its output using fgets(). Also demonstrate how to use pclose() to get the command's exit status.
keyPoints: Include necessary headers: stdio.h, stdlib.h
Use popen("ls -l", "r") to open pipe for reading
Use fgets() in a loop to read command output
Print each line of output
Use pclose() to close pipe and get exit status
Use WEXITSTATUS to extract and print exit code
Proper error handling for popen() failure
Close pipe and handle errors properly

questions.question: Write a C program that uses popen() to send data to a shell command (e.g., mail or wc -l) and properly close the pipe using pclose(). Explain the flow.
keyPoints: Open pipe in 'w' mode using popen()
Write data to pipe using fprintf() or fputs()
Close pipe using pclose() - important to close BEFORE checking status
Check pclose() return value for errors
Extract exit status using WEXITSTATUS macro
Explain why pclose() must be called after all writes

questions.question: Explain the complete process lifecycle when popen() and pclose() are used. Include fork(), exec(), pipe operations, and process termination in your explanation.
keyPoints: Parent calls popen() which creates a pipe
popen() forks a child process
Child: closes unused end, redirects stdout/stdin, execs /bin/sh -c command
Parent: gets FILE pointer to read/write
Data flows through pipe between parent and child
Parent calls pclose() to close pipe
pclose() calls waitpid() to wait for child
Child terminates, parent collects exit status
Parent returns exit status to caller
Zombie prevention is key benefit

questions.question: Compare popen()/pclose() with the traditional pipe(), fork(), exec() approach for process communication. List advantages and disadvantages of each approach.
keyPoints: Traditional: more control, explicit pipe setup, fork, exec needed
popen: simpler API, automatic shell handling, single function call
popen advantages: easier to use, shell expansion, automatic zombie handling
Traditional advantages: more control, no shell overhead, better performance for simple cases
popen disadvantages: shell spawn overhead, less control over process
Traditional disadvantages: more code, manual zombie prevention, no shell features
